source.title: Anticholinergic drugs and risk of dementia: case-control study

confidence_rationale: Large case-control study (40,770 dementia cases, 283,933 controls) from UK general practices (Clinical Practice Research Datalink) with drug exposure measured 4-20 years before diagnosis. Found adjusted OR of 1.11 (95% CI 1.08-1.14) for any anticholinergic drug, with class-specific differences: antidepressants, urological drugs, and antiparkinson drugs showed increased dementia risk; gastrointestinal anticholinergics did not. The long latency (15-20 years before diagnosis) and class specificity support MECH-175's dual-pathway account. Confidence moderate-high: very large sample, long exposure window, published in BMJ. Limitations: observational design (indication bias: older adults prescribed anticholinergics may have higher baseline dementia risk for independent reasons); class specificity could reflect indication confounding (antidepressant use = depression = dementia risk factor).
failure_signatures: Indication bias is a serious concern: patients prescribed antidepressants (which have high anticholinergic burden in some classes) may have depression, which is itself a dementia risk factor, making it impossible to isolate the anticholinergic-specific pathway.

The study does not distinguish the nocturnal REM suppression pathway (MECH-175 Pathway 1) from the diurnal cholinergic deficit pathway (MECH-175 Pathway 2) -- it cannot test which mechanism mediates the observed association.

Gastrointestinal anticholinergics (e.g. hyoscine) showed no increased risk despite high anticholinergic burden, which could suggest either: (a) poor CNS penetration limits the nocturnal effect, or (b) indication confounding is driving the positive associations for other drug classes rather than the anticholinergic mechanism itself.

mapping.source_claim_statement: 35% of dementia cases vs 30% of controls received anticholinergic drugs with definite activity. Adjusted OR 1.11 (1.08-1.14) for any anticholinergic drug. Class differences: antidepressants and urological drugs associated with increased dementia risk; gastrointestinal drugs not. Long latency (exposure 15-20 years before diagnosis) persists.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-175 predicts anticholinergic burden confers dementia risk via two independent pathways: Pathway 1 (nocturnal REM suppression degrading MECH-123 Phase 4 precision recalibration) and Pathway 2 (diurnal cholinergic deficit mimicking NBM lesion, disrupting waking contextual attribution via basal forebrain cholinergic projection to cortex). The long latency finding (15-20 years before diagnosis) directly supports the mechanism being causal and cumulative, not merely a prodrome marker. The class specificity (antidepressants > GI anticholinergics) is consistent with CNS-penetrating agents having greater pipeline access, though the GI exception could also reflect indication confounding.
mapping.mapping_caveat: This study provides epidemiological support for the anticholinergic-dementia association but cannot distinguish the two MECH-175 pathways (nocturnal vs diurnal). It also cannot rule out indication confounding as an alternative explanation for the class-specific findings. The long latency (15-20 years) is consistent with cumulative pipeline disruption but is also consistent with many other causal models. MECH-175 requires the dual-pathway mechanism to be correct for the association to be mechanistically explained -- the epidemiology is consistent but not pathway-specific.
mapping.source_context: UK Clinical Practice Research Datalink case-control study; 40,770 dementia cases; drug exposure 4-20 years before diagnosis; published BMJ 2018.

confidence_components.notes: Excellent source quality: very large sample, major journal, long exposure window. Moderate mapping fidelity: consistent with MECH-175 but cannot distinguish pathways or rule out indication confounding. Lower transfer risk than other entries because the population (older adults, long-term medication use) matches MECH-175's target context well.
